Scenario.title: Unitronics Defacement Campaign Targeting Water/Wastewater Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack leveraging AI-driven reconnaissance, wireless exploitation, and credential theft to disrupt critical infrastructure operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT), Web Browser, Internet-facing assets, Command, Windows Registry, OS Process, User Account, Process, Domain Name Registrar, File, Windows Service
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Exploit via Radio Interfaces, General Access Method, Command Execution (Mechanism), Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), Exploits, Process Injection, User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Reg.exe Utility, Pass the Hash, File Modification (Mechanism), Process Creation (Mechanism), Systemd Service

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers deploy AI algorithms to scrape public records, social media, and technical documentation to map the water/wastewater system's infrastructure, identifying vulnerable IoT devices and personnel associated with maintenance protocols.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The threat actors develop custom exploit kits targeting radio-frequency vulnerabilities in industrial control systems (ICS), creating rogue Wi-Fi access points to intercept unencrypted communications between operators and SCADA systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: A spear-phishing campaign delivers malicious PDF attachments to engineers, exploiting outdated Adobe Reader vulnerabilities to execute arbitrary code and establish a foothold in the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-9: Buffer Overflow in Local Command-Line Utilities
stage.description: The attackers chain buffer overflow exploits in command-line utilities to run payloads that disable firewall rules and enable lateral movement across segmented networks.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A scheduled task is created in the Windows Registry to maintain persistent access, automatically re-establishing compromised sessions after system reboots.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Using OSINT-derived credentials, the attackers exploit hardcoded admin passwords in legacy ICS software to elevate privileges and access restricted subsystems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legitimate system processes to evade detection by security software while maintaining persistence.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: The attackers automate password spraying attacks against domain controllers, leveraging weak administrative passwords to harvest valid credentials.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network sniffing tools like Reg.exe are used to monitor internal traffic, mapping out active systems, open ports, and potential targets for further exploitation.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Stolen credentials are used with pass-the-hash techniques to move laterally across the network, accessing additional systems and databases.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Critical operational data is exfiltrated and destroyed by corrupting configuration files and database entries, disrupting water treatment processes.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Data is exfiltrated through covert tunnels created by manipulating process creation mechanisms, masking traffic patterns to avoid detection.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: A malicious systemd service is installed to alter pump controls and valve settings, causing physical damage to infrastructure and environmental hazards.

Scenario.summary: The Unitronics defacement campaign systematically compromises water/wastewater systems through AI-powered reconnaissance, wireless exploitation, and credential theft, culminating in operational disruption and physical damage via manipulated industrial controls.
